verses.commentary: The law is only a shadow of the good things coming, not the realities themselves. Shadows reveal shape but lack substance. The law pointed forward but could never perfect those who drew near. Repeated sacrifices proved their inadequacy.

Otherwise, would they not have stopped being offered? If sacrifices truly cleansed, they would cease. But their continuation proved their insufficiency. Worshipers would have been cleansed once for all and felt no more guilt.

But those sacrifices are an annual reminder of sins. Far from removing guilt, sacrifices reminded worshipers of sin every year. The Day of Atonement memorialized failure rather than accomplishing forgiveness.
It is impossible for the blood of bulls and goats to take away sins. Animal blood cannot atone for human sin. Different nature, insufficient value. The old system was typological, not effectual.

When Christ came into the world, He said: Sacrifice and offering You did not desire, but a body You prepared for Me. Psalm 40:6-8 quoted. God's ultimate desire was not ritual but obedience. The incarnation was necessary to accomplish what animals could not.
With burnt offerings and sin offerings You were not pleased. God took no pleasure in the sacrificial system as an end in itself. It pointed beyond itself to Christ's willing obedience.
Then I said, Here I am, I have come to do Your will, my God. Christ's incarnation was purposeful: to do the Father's will. Submission, not ritual, accomplishes redemption.

First He said, Sacrifices and offerings You did not desire, nor were You pleased with them (though they were offered in accordance with the law). Even Law-prescribed sacrifices were insufficient. They were good but not ultimate.

Then He said, Here I am, I have come to do Your will. He sets aside the first to establish the second. The first covenant is set aside; the second replaces it. Christ's obedience supersedes ceremonial ritual.

And by that will, we have been made holy through the sacrifice of the body of Jesus Christ once for all. Sanctification accomplished through Christ's physical death. Once for all (ephapax) again emphasized. No repetition needed or possible.

Day after day every priest stands and performs his religious duties; again and again he offers the same sacrifices, which can never take away sins. Standing indicates incomplete work. Repetition reveals insufficiency. Levitical priests never finished.

But when this priest had offered for all time one sacrifice for sins, He sat down at the right hand of God. Sitting indicates completed work. Christ's single sacrifice accomplished what countless animal sacrifices could not.
And since that time He waits for His enemies to be made His footstool. Psalm 110:1 again. Christ's work of atonement is complete; His work of subjugation continues until all enemies are defeated.

For by one sacrifice He has made perfect forever those who are being made holy. One sacrifice perfects positionally (justification) while progressive sanctification continues. Perfection is both accomplished (once) and applied (ongoing).
The Holy Spirit also testifies to us about this. First He says: This introduces Jeremiah 31:33-34 again. The new covenant promise is Spirit-testified truth.

This is the covenant I will make with them: I will put My laws in their hearts, and write them on their minds. Internal transformation characterizes the new covenant. Obedience flows from new nature, not external compulsion.

Then He adds: Their sins and lawless acts I will remember no more. God's choice not to remember sins is the foundation of the new covenant. Not amnesia but gracious forgetting—He chooses not to hold sins against us.
And where these have been forgiven, sacrifice for sin is no longer necessary. Forgiveness is complete; additional sacrifice is superfluous. Christ's work needs no supplement.

Therefore, brothers and sisters, since we have confidence to enter the Most Holy Place by the blood of Jesus. Transition to exhortation. Confidence (parrēsia) to enter God's presence is the privilege of believers. Access once restricted is now open.
By a new and living way opened for us through the curtain, that is, His body. The torn curtain at Christ's death symbolized open access. His torn body is the way into God's presence.
And since we have a great priest over the house of God. Christ's priesthood and our access are inseparable. He is both the way and the priest who leads us into God's presence.

Let us draw near to God with a sincere heart and full assurance of faith, having our hearts sprinkled to cleanse us from a guilty conscience and bodies washed with pure water. Three exhortations begin here. First: draw near. Worship, prayer, and fellowship with God are now possible. Cleansing is both spiritual (hearts sprinkled) and symbolic (bodies washed, likely baptism).

Let us hold unswervingly to the hope we profess, for He who promised is faithful. Second exhortation: hold fast. Perseverance is grounded in God's faithfulness, not ours. Hope is secure because God is trustworthy.

And let us consider how we may spur one another on toward love and good deeds. Third exhortation: provoke one another to love. Mutual encouragement is essential. Believers need one another to persevere and grow.

Not giving up meeting together, as some are in the habit of doing, but encouraging one another—and all the more as you see the Day approaching. Corporate worship is not optional. Isolation breeds spiritual danger. As Christ's return nears, gathering becomes more urgent.

If we deliberately keep on sinning after receiving the knowledge of the truth, no sacrifice for sins is left. Willful, persistent sin after full knowledge is apostasy. Not struggling with sin, but deliberate rejection of Christ. No other sacrifice exists; rejecting Christ's sacrifice leaves no remedy.

But only a fearful expectation of judgment and of raging fire that will consume the enemies of God. Apostates face judgment, not mercy. They are counted among God's enemies. The fire is eschatological judgment.
Anyone who rejected the law of Moses died without mercy on the testimony of two or three witnesses. Deuteronomy 17:6 cited. The old covenant prescribed capital punishment for willful rebellion.

How much more severely do you think someone deserves to be punished who has trampled the Son of God underfoot, treated as unholy the blood of the covenant that sanctified them, and insulted the Spirit of grace? If rejecting Moses brought death, how much worse to reject Christ? Three heinous acts: trampling the Son, profaning His blood, insulting the Spirit. This is willful, informed apostasy.

For we know Him who said, It is Mine to avenge; I will repay, and again, The Lord will judge His people. Deuteronomy 32:35-36 quoted. Vengeance belongs to God. He will judge even His professing people if they apostatize.

It is a dreadful thing to fall into the hands of the living God. Not a comforting God but a living, holy, just God. Apostasy brings not annihilation but judgment. Falling into His hands as an enemy is terrifying.

Remember those earlier days after you had received the light, when you endured in a great conflict full of suffering. Encouragement follows warning. They persevered before; they can persevere again. Past faithfulness encourages present endurance.

Sometimes you were publicly exposed to insult and persecution; at other times you stood side by side with those who were so treated. They suffered both personally and vicariously, identifying with persecuted believers. Solidarity in suffering marks genuine faith.

You suffered along with those in prison and joyfully accepted the confiscation of your property, because you knew you had better and lasting possessions. Sacrificial love for imprisoned believers. Joyful acceptance of loss because of eternal perspective. Earthly possessions pale beside heavenly treasure.
So do not throw away your confidence; it will be richly rewarded. Confidence (parrēsia) is precious. Do not discard what cost so much and promises so much. Reward awaits those who persevere.

You need to persevere so that when you have done the will of God, you will receive what He has promised. Endurance is necessary to obtain the promise. Quitting forfeits reward. Perseverance proves genuine faith.

For, In just a little while, He who is coming will come and will not delay. Isaiah 26:20 and Habakkuk 2:3-4 blended. Christ's return is imminent. What feels long is actually short from God's perspective. He will come; He will not delay.

And, But My righteous one will live by faith. And I take no pleasure in the one who shrinks back. Faith is the mode of righteous living. Shrinking back displeases God. Faith perseveres; apostasy retreats.

But we do not belong to those who shrink back and are destroyed, but to those who have faith and are saved. Final word of assurance. The writer is confident his readers are genuinely saved and will persevere. Faith saves; apostasy destroys.
